title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Market Experienced Falling Type 1 Trade Volumes Alongwith Slight Dips in Prices
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(27th January â€“ 2nd February)
  • A total of 294,105 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 61.60% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades decreased by 40.70% WoW as 746,105 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $69.92/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 2.52%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 0.88% in price, reaching $70.70/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 2.96% WoW; On 5th Feb, the spot price stood at $73.50/MT. The benchmark price stood at $77.48/MT on 5th
  • D3 RIN prices increased by 0.84% WoW, D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 2.82% and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.41% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

This week saw a decline in Type 1 trade volumes and prices within the CA LCFS market, with volumes experiencing notable WoW drops. Non-zero trades also saw a reduction in activity. Despite this, the average spot credit price increased slightly, indicating some upward movement in the market. In the RFS market, there was a general upward trend across all categories, with D3, D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices all witnessing modest increases. In the USA, the focus on biofuels has been a key concern area since the new year began, with several legislative and policy developments highlighting the importance of biofuels like ethanol. In North Dakota, a new bill seeks to establish a low-carbon fuels fund to assist ethanol producers in reducing carbon intensity, boosting local corn demand, and tapping into the growing sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market. Similarly, Kansas has proposed HB 2012, which offers a tax credit for retail dealers and distributors selling higher ethanol blends (15%-85%), incentivizing greater ethanol use. At the federal level, Congresswoman Nikki Budzinski has championed initiatives to support the biofuels industry, including strengthening the 45Z Clean Fuels Tax Credit for producing low-carbon transportation fuels like ethanol and biodiesel. Additionally, the IRS has announced forthcoming proposed regulations on the clean fuel production credit under Â§ 45Z, as outlined in Notice 2025-10, with a request for public comments. Notice 2025-11 also provides initial guidance on emissions rates for the credit.Â 

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_591956" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 2.52% WoW fall, decreasing from $71.73 to $69.92 this week, with all non-zero credit prices decreased by 0.88% WoW, falling from $71.33 to $70.70. Type 1 trades saw 294,105 credits exchanged, reflecting a 61.60% WoW decrease. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades decreased by 40.70% WoW to reach 746,105 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_591957" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 2.96% WoW, reaching $71.20/MT. On 5th February, the average spot price was $73.50/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $77.48/MT on 5th February.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_591958" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 73 trades were reported in the market, with four high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $166.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 12,500 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 50,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $71.88, whereas the smallest transfer volume was reported for a transaction of 6 credits of the Type 1 category at $68.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_591959" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In January, the credit volume stood at 6.72 MMT, slightly lower than December's volume of 7.38 MMT. Meanwhile, the Type 1 average spot price saw a modest increase, rising from $71.89 in December to $72.56 in January. The trading volumes exhibited a significant year-over-year (YOY) growth, reflecting a 31.25% increase from the 5.12 MMT recorded in January of the prior year.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_591960" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 27th Jan â€“ 2nd Feb, total VPT recorded a 1.45% decrease (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 0.84% WoW, D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 2.82%and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.41% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Trump Administration Asks Supreme Court to Pause Cases As it Reconsiders Policy President Donald Trump's administration asked the U.S. Supreme Court on Friday to put on hold planned arguments in a bid by fuel producers to challenge California's standards for vehicle emissions and electric cars under a federal air pollution law. The dispute over California vehicle standards centers on an exception granted to that state in 2022 by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency during Biden's presidency to national vehicle emission standards set by the agency under the landmark Clean Air Act anti-pollution law.
  • North Dakota Legislation Aims to Help Ethanol Producers Lower Their CI Scores A new bill introduced in the North Dakota Senate seeks to establish a low-carbon fuels fund to help the state's ethanol producers reduce the carbon intensity (CI) of their fuel, boosting local corn demand and enabling producers to benefit from the growing sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market.
  • Kansas Bill Proposes 5-Cent Tax Credit for Higher Ethanol Blends from 2026 to 2031 The proposed Kansas bill, HB 2012, outlines a tax credit for retail dealers and distributors selling higher ethanol blends (15%-85% ethanol) from 2026 to 2031. Retail dealers and distributors can claim a $0.05 per gallon credit, capped at $5 million per year. Unused credits can be carried forward for up to five years, but the credit is non-refundable.
  • EnviTecâ€™s 23 Years of Expertise Optimize Chicken Manure for RNG in the US Market Germany's largest biogas producer, EnviTec Biogas AG, has been leveraging chicken manure for the production of biogas, renewable natural gas (RNG), and bio-Liquified Natural Gas (LNG) for an impressive 23 years. The company is now actively exploring opportunities in the growing U.S. market for the anaerobic digestion of poultry manure, aligning its expertise with the increasing demand for sustainable waste-to-energy solutions.
